planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, deep, sandy-loam soil in full sun; African locust bean (dawadawa or nere) is one of the most important food trees of West Africa — the seeds are fermented into dawadawa, one of sub-Saharan Africa's most important and most pungent flavor condiments (similar in culinary function to fish sauce, miso, or soy sauce), while the sweet, yellow, powdery pulp around the seed pods is eaten fresh and used as a sweetener.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, deep, sandy-loam to clay-loam soil in full sun; African locust bean is drought-tolerant once established and fixes nitrogen. Parkia biglobosa (African locust bean, dawadawa, or nere) is a massive, wide-canopied leguminous tree from the West African savanna and Sudan zone — one of the most economically and nutritionally important trees across a broad band of West Africa from Senegal to Ethiopia. The tree produces striking, spherical, red to orange pompom flower heads on pendulous stalks — spectacularly ornamental. The fruits are long pods containing seeds embedded in a dry, yellow, powdery, sweet pulp. The sweet pulp (high in sugar, vitamin C, and tannins) is eaten fresh or dissolved in water as a sweet drink. The seeds are the critical product: fermented over several days into dawadawa (called iru in Yoruba, ogiri-nono in Igbo, sumbala in Mande-speaking regions) — an intensely aromatic, deeply umami-flavored condiment with a pungent smell similar to very strong blue cheese or natto. Dawadawa is the essential flavoring in West African soups, stews, and sauces in the same way that fish sauce is essential in Southeast Asia — providing deep umami richness. The tree also fixes atmospheric nitrogen (Rhizobium symbiosis), improving soil fertility. A supremely important multi-use tree for tropical food forests in zone 10–12.
sources: FAO — Parkia biglobosa: A Multipurpose African Tree
ICRAF World Agroforestry Centre — Parkia biglobosa
International Journal of Food Sciences — Dawadawa Production

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, deep, sandy-loam soil in full sun; shea tree is a deep-rooted, drought-tolerant, extremely slow-growing savanna tree of West and Central Africa — deeply sacred in West African cultures and one of the most economically important trees on the continent, the source of shea butter (karite butter), the dominant vegetable fat of sub-Saharan Africa and a global cosmetics industry ingredient; the sweet, fleshy fruits are also edible.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, deep, sandy-loam soil in full sun; extremely drought-tolerant once established — shea is a true savanna tree adapted to seasonal drought; requires decades to reach full production. Vitellaria paradoxa (shea tree or karite) is one of the most culturally and economically important trees in sub-Saharan Africa — a long-lived (200–300 years), slow-growing deciduous tree native to the dry savanna and Sudan zone from West Africa east to Uganda and Ethiopia. For millennia, shea has been a tree of extraordinary importance: the large seeds (shea nuts) yield shea fat (shea butter or karite butter), a solid, ivory-colored fat with exceptional cosmetic properties — outstanding skin and hair moisturizing ability, high stearic acid content, resistance to rancidity — that has become one of the most sought-after cosmetic ingredients worldwide and is also a major cooking fat and chocolate substitute in West African cuisine. Shea butter is one of the primary cooking fats across the West African Sahel. The sweet, green, plum-like fleshy fruit surrounding the shea nut is also edible fresh — tasting mildly sweet with a date-like softness. The tree is considered sacred in many West African cultures and is legally protected in several countries — it may not be cut down without authorization. Almost never cultivated outside Africa due to its extremely slow growth and extremely long time to first production (15–30 years from seed); a truly generational investment tree.
sources: ICRAF World Agroforestry Centre — Vitellaria paradoxa
Global Shea Alliance — Shea Tree Biology and Production

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in very well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral, sandy to loamy, dryland soil in full sun; desert date is an extremely drought-tolerant, thorny, small tree of the African and Middle Eastern drylands — producing oval, olive-sized, yellow-brown fruits with sweet-bitter, date-like flesh around a hard seed — one of the most drought-resistant multi-use trees in the Sahel, used across the semi-arid belt of Africa from Senegal to Ethiopia and into the Middle East.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral, sandy to loamy dryland soil in full sun; one of the most drought-tolerant fruiting trees available — tolerates 3–6 months of complete drought once established. Balanites aegyptiaca (desert date, heglig, or soap berry tree) is a thorny, slow-growing tree native to the arid and semi-arid regions of sub-Saharan Africa, the Sahel, and the Middle East — one of the most widely distributed and ecologically important trees of the African drylands, found from Senegal and Mauritania east through Sudan and Ethiopia and into the Arabian Peninsula. The tree produces oval, olive-sized, yellow-brown to dark-brown fruits (drupes) with fleshy, oily mesocarp that is sweet-bitter with a date-like character; the fruits are eaten fresh, used to flavor traditional porridges, fermented into beer, and pressed for oil. The seed kernel yields an edible oil used in cooking and as a skin preparation. The bark, leaves, and fruit rind contain saponins traditionally used as soap — hence 'soap berry tree' — and in traditional fish stunning for fishing (the saponins narcotize fish). The tree has extensive traditional medicinal applications across Africa (treatment of malaria, diabetes, skin conditions) and is increasingly studied for bioactive compounds. One of the premier trees for the dryland agroforestry systems of the Sahel, where it withstands extreme drought, poor soils, and degraded conditions that defeat most other trees.

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in moist, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, deep, loamy soil in full sun to part shade; dattock or sweet detar is a large West African forest tree producing small, round, date-like fruits with sweet-acid, edible brown flesh that is exceptionally rich in vitamin C — one of the sweetest and most pleasant wild fruits of the West African coastal forests, eaten fresh throughout Senegal, Guinea, and Sierra Leone.

directions: Plant in moist, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, deep, loamy soil in full sun to part shade; dattock is a forest tree that benefits from some shade protection when young. Detarium senegalense (dattock or sweet detar) is a tall leguminous tree from the coastal forests, gallery forests, and moist woodland savannas of West Africa — native across Senegal, Guinea-Bissau, Guinea, Sierra Leone, Ivory Coast, Ghana, and Nigeria. The tree produces round to slightly flattened, 1–1.5 inch, thin-shelled fruits containing large, flat, hard seeds embedded in sweet, brown, fibrous, edible mesocarp with a distinctive sweet-acid, almost tamarind-like flavor. The fruits are exceptionally rich in vitamin C (up to 100 mg per 100g — roughly twice that of orange juice), and are eaten fresh as a pleasant sweet-sour snack across rural West Africa. The hard seeds contain a starchy meal used in traditional foods. The tree yields a useful timber and a resin used in traditional medicine. In Senegal, the sweet detar fruits are particularly popular and are sold in local markets. Sweet detar (D. senegalense) is the sweet-fruited species, distinguished from bitter detar (D. microcarpum), which has similar fruits but with an unpleasantly bitter taste — an important distinction for edibility. A largely unknown and underexplored fruit tree outside West Africa.
sources: FAO Non-Wood Forest Products — Detarium senegalense
PROTA — Plant Resources of Tropical Africa
Kew Royal Botanic Gardens — Plants of the World Online

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring or fall in well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral, sandy to loamy soil in full sun to part shade; russet buffaloberry is a native Canadian and northern US shrub — one of the most cold-hardy native berry shrubs on the continent — producing red-orange to yellow, translucent, bitter berries that are edible only after frost or cooking; its unique saponin content was traditionally exploited by indigenous peoples to whip the berries into a frothy 'Indian ice cream' or 'soopalallie.'

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; extremely cold-hardy and drought-tolerant once established; the plant fixes nitrogen through Frankia bacterial symbiosis. Shepherdia canadensis (russet buffaloberry or soopalallie) is a deciduous native shrub of Canada, Alaska, and the northern United States — one of the most cold-hardy native fruiting shrubs on the continent, thriving in zone 2–6 conditions where few fruiting plants survive. The plant produces small (1/4-inch), translucent, red-orange to yellow, elliptic berries in summer that are bitter and astringent when eaten raw due to saponin content, but become edible after frost or cooking. The most famous traditional use is as 'sxusem' or 'soopalallie' (in Chinook jargon) — the indigenous peoples of British Columbia and the Pacific Northwest whipped the raw berries with water to produce a frothy, bright-red, bitter-sweet foam similar to meringue, traditionally sweetened with berries or honey and eaten as 'Indian ice cream.' The high saponin content is what enables the foam to form — the same compounds that make dish soap foam. The berries are also used for jelly, syrup, and beverages. Shephaerdia canadensis is a dioecious nitrogen-fixer (requires separate male and female plants, and fixes nitrogen through Frankia actinorrhizal symbiosis), making it valuable in cold-climate food forest systems as a soil-building shrub with an edible berry crop.
sources: University of Idaho Extension — Native Shrubs

planting.note: Plant rooted divisions in spring or fall in moist to wet, peaty, slightly acidic to neutral, poor, boggy soil in full sun to part shade; bog myrtle is a native aromatic shrub of peat bogs, wet heathlands, and lakeshores of North America and northern Europe — the strongly fragrant, resinous leaves were the primary bittering and flavoring agent in European ale before hops became dominant (medieval gruit ale), and are still used to flavor gin, whisky, and craft beers.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, peaty, slightly acidic to neutral, poor, boggy soil in full sun to part shade; bog myrtle requires consistently moist to wet conditions and does not tolerate drought; fixes nitrogen through Frankia actinorrhizal symbiosis. Myrica gale (bog myrtle or sweet gale) is a deciduous aromatic shrub native to the peat bogs, wet heathlands, and lakeshores of northern North America, northern Europe, and northern Asia — found from Labrador and Maine across Canada to Alaska, and from Ireland and Scotland east through Scandinavia and Russia. The small, lance-shaped, blue-green leaves are intensely aromatic when crushed — a warm, sweet, resinous, herbal fragrance combining elements of bay, eucalyptus, and spice — that has been exploited for culinary and brewing purposes for thousands of years. Before hops (Humulus lupulus) became the universal bittering agent in European beer in the late Middle Ages, bog myrtle (along with other herbs in the 'gruit' mixture) was the primary flavoring of European ale — recorded in historical documents as far back as the 5th century CE. Gruit ale is currently experiencing a major revival in craft brewing, with bog myrtle used in traditional Scottish, Scandinavian, and craft brewery gruit beers. The leaves are also used to flavor Scandinavian aquavit, Scottish whisky cocktails, and gin botanicals. The plant naturally spreads by rhizomes in wet soils and fixes nitrogen through Frankia bacterial symbiosis. A unique and historically significant herb plant for pond edges and bog gardens in zone 1–6.
sources: American Botanical Council — Bog Myrtle in Brewing History
